startup.title: The startup handshake
startup.body: What you just watched is the connection coming alive. The client opens with a StartupMessage stating who it is and which database it wants; the server replies with an Authentication challenge (or AuthenticationOK if no auth is required); after credentials clear, the server volunteers a flurry of ParameterStatus messages and a BackendKeyData, then declares itself idle with ReadyForQuery. The ParameterStatus messages are unsolicited — the server tells the client about settings it should know (server_version, client_encoding, application_name, …) so the client never has to ask. Once you see ReadyForQuery(I), the connection is open and the first user-level command can be sent.

scram_exchange.title: SCRAM-SHA-256 in three legs
scram_exchange.body: SCRAM is a mutual-authentication protocol. The server says "prove who you are using one of these mechanisms" (AuthenticationSASL), the client picks SCRAM-SHA-256 and sends a client-first message containing a random nonce (SASLResponse). The server responds with its own nonce, a salt, and an iteration count (AuthenticationSASLContinue) — the client uses these to derive a proof that it knows the password without ever sending it. The client returns that proof (SASLResponse); the server verifies it and sends back its own proof (AuthenticationSASLFinal) so the client knows it's talking to the real server. Only then does AuthenticationOK appear.

extended_query_batch.title: Pipelining the extended-query protocol
extended_query_batch.body: Notice that the client just sent Parse, Bind, Describe, Execute, and Sync back-to-back without waiting for a single reply. This is the whole point of the extended-query protocol: the client fires the entire batch and then reads the five responses in order (ParseComplete, BindComplete, RowDescription/NoData, then DataRows + CommandComplete, then ReadyForQuery). Compare this with a simple Query message, which is one round trip with a streaming reply — the extended protocol trades simplicity for the ability to send parameters out-of-band, reuse prepared statements, and request binary result formats. Sync at the end is what tells the server "that's the batch; reply with ReadyForQuery and resync error state."

simple_query_cycle.title: The simplest request/response
simple_query_cycle.body: This is the simple-query protocol at its most basic: one Query message carrying a SQL string, one RowDescription describing the columns, a stream of DataRow messages (one per row), a CommandComplete with the result tag ("SELECT 3"), and finally a ReadyForQuery to say the server is idle again. No preparation, no parameters, no cursor. If the same query were sent through the extended-query protocol you'd see five messages outbound and many more on the way back — simple-query trades flexibility for fewer messages on the wire.

error_recovery.title: How the server recovers from errors
error_recovery.body: Whenever the server sends ErrorResponse, it then sends a ReadyForQuery to tell the client that any in-flight statement has been abandoned and the connection is ready for the next command. In the middle of an extended-query batch (Parse/Bind/Describe/Execute), the server skips every remaining message until it sees a Sync — that's why Sync exists: to mark a re-sync point after an error. If you ever wondered why your driver eats the rest of a batch after one bad statement, this is the rule.
